claims.normalized_claim: In Gideon v. Wainwright (1963) the Supreme Court held that the Sixth Amendment right to the assistance of counsel is a fundamental right, made obligatory on the states by the Fourteenth Amendment, so a state must provide appointed counsel to a defendant charged with a serious offense who cannot afford a lawyer.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court (Black, J.), holding
allowed_wording: The Court ruled that states must provide a lawyer to poor defendants who can't afford one.
The right to a lawyer applies to the states, and the state must appoint one if you can't pay.
prohibited_wording: Gideon guaranteed a free lawyer for every legal matter.
Gideon created the right to remain silent.

claims.normalized_claim: Gideon v. Wainwright was decided on March 18, 1963 by a unanimous Court, with the opinion of the Court written by Justice Hugo Black.
counterevidence: Justices Douglas, Clark, and Harlan filed concurring opinions; the result (right to counsel applies to the states) was unanimous.
allowed_wording: In 1963 a unanimous Court, in an opinion by Justice Hugo Black, ruled for Gideon.
prohibited_wording: The Court was deeply divided on the result.
claims.notes: Date / unanimity / author. Concurrences existed but the result was unanimous.

claims.normalized_claim: Gideon v. Wainwright expressly overruled Betts v. Brady (1942), which had held that appointment of counsel in state non-capital cases was required only where special circumstances made a fair trial otherwise impossible.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court (treatment of Betts v. Brady)
allowed_wording: Gideon overturned an earlier ruling, Betts v. Brady, that had let states deny poor defendants a lawyer.
prohibited_wording: There was no prior rule on this question.
claims.notes: The precedent overruled; the obstacle Gideon had to clear.

claims.normalized_claim: Clarence Earl Gideon was charged in Florida with breaking and entering with intent to commit a misdemeanor (a felony under Florida law); at his 1961 trial he asked the court to appoint a lawyer, the judge refused because Florida then appointed counsel only in capital cases, Gideon represented himself, was convicted, and was sentenced to prison.
allowed_wording: Charged with breaking into a Florida poolroom, Gideon asked for a lawyer, was refused, defended himself, and was convicted.
prohibited_wording: Gideon was charged with a violent crime.
claims.notes: Real person; state the charge and outcome accurately (docs/32). He maintained innocence and was later acquitted on retrial (CLM-0007).

claims.normalized_claim: From prison, Gideon filed a handwritten petition asking the Supreme Court to hear his case, proceeding in forma pauperis (as a poor person who could not pay court fees).
allowed_wording: Gideon mailed the Supreme Court a handwritten petition from his prison cell.
prohibited_wording: A team of lawyers brought Gideon's case to the Supreme Court.
claims.notes: The handwritten in forma pauperis petition is the iconic fact; graded B (procedural record / institutional source).

claims.normalized_claim: The Supreme Court appointed Abe Fortas, a prominent Washington attorney (later a Supreme Court Justice), to represent Gideon before the Court.
evidence_locations: Oyez: counsel of record / case record
allowed_wording: The Court appointed Abe Fortas to argue Gideon's side.
claims.notes: The irony: the man denied a lawyer was finally given one of the best in the country, by the Court itself.

claims.normalized_claim: After the Supreme Court reversed his conviction, Gideon was retried in 1963 with an appointed lawyer and was acquitted.
allowed_wording: Retried with a real lawyer, Gideon was found not guilty.
prohibited_wording: Gideon went back to prison after the ruling.
claims.notes: The payoff contrast with Miranda: Gideon actually won his freedom on retrial.

claims.normalized_claim: The right vindicated in Gideon is grounded in the Sixth Amendment guarantee of assistance of counsel, applied to the states through the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court, constitutional basis
allowed_wording: The ruling rests on the Sixth Amendment right to a lawyer, made binding on the states by the Fourteenth Amendment.
prohibited_wording: The ruling is based on the Fifth Amendment.
The ruling is based on the Second Amendment.
claims.notes: Constitutional basis; distinct from Miranda's Fifth Amendment grounding.

claims.normalized_claim: Gideon is widely credited with driving the expansion of appointed-counsel and public-defender systems across the United States, while the resources to fund that right (staffing, caseloads) have remained a persistent strain.
allowed_wording: Gideon is why public defender offices exist across the country, even as those offices are often stretched thin.
prohibited_wording: Every defendant today gets a fully resourced lawyer.
Public defenders handle X cases per year (no specific number is sourced).
claims.notes: Impact / 'imperfect system' angle. Keep qualitative; no unsourced numbers (rule 09). Specific funding/caseload statistics would be E-grade and are excluded.

claims.normalized_claim: The Miranda warning's promise that 'if you cannot afford a lawyer, one will be appointed for you' depends on the right to appointed counsel established in Gideon.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court (right to appointed counsel)
allowed_wording: The fourth Miranda warning only means something because Gideon guaranteed the appointed lawyer it promises.
prohibited_wording: Gideon and Miranda are the same case.
Miranda created the right to a lawyer.
claims.notes: Series-thread link to EP1. Framed as the logical/legal dependency, not a quote.

findings: Live source verification pending: SRC-0001/0002/0003 content_hash + verified_at not yet captured. run_research.py is Miranda-specific; generalize the research adapter (per-case cluster/Oyez URL) and run with COURTLISTENER_TOKEN to flip warn->pass before publish.
CLM-0005/0007 graded B from institutional source (Oyez) + docket record; acceptable for script framing, confirm against primary record at verification.
